speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're on tape seven with Rachela Warszauer Schlufman, August 29, 1995. Mrs. Schlufman, you were telling me about your sister and your nieces going.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister actually was forced to come to Skarzysko-Kamienna while the evacuation took place on some historic day. That was on Sunday. And like I mentioned to you, she was brought from [POLISH] to Skarzysko. She stood with her two children and my parents and Moishe and Shmulek together to be evacuated. Well, Bartenschlater came from camp, from my camp when I was there already. And he wanted to take out some new people, you know, from the evacuation.

speaker: interviewer
text: New workers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was there when he came--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, I'm saying that he was looking for new workers.

speaker: interviewee
text: For new workers. So whom did he pick up? He picked up my Tobcha. He picked up Tobcha. But of course, Tobcha was young yet, you know? And she was the age of 26, and pretty, and healthy looking yet. Even she stood-- she was for two weeks laying there in the bunker.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was holding her children. Would she leave her children? But he started to whip her with his whip over her head. She got scared. She says, mein kinder, mein-- So she called her-- she said to my mother, who they stood all together, Ma, please take care of my children. And he took her out, took her out with many others. When I came back from work, I met them all, that group who came from the, you know, from the evacuation. They were the people from the evacuation. The best ones, he picked them up. So that's what my sister told me how it was, about the evacuation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister-- the children went with the grandparents, with my parents. And Shmulek, my mother pushed him away. He was near the train, you know. But my mother pushed him away. He should meet-- to go to Tobcha. But he was a little boy. He was short. And he was afraid. And somehow he sneaked in. And when they started to count, the group, they didn't see how little he was. And he came into the camp. And he was on work there. But after working-- Shmulek worked there for a while, very capable, young boy, very smart, until now very smart and very proud of him, of my dear brother. And he looked around, and he did see that he's not going to survive. But the faces were yellow from that [INAUDIBLE], what they were using, the powder for the ammunition work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the people could not last long. Their faces got yellow, affected their lungs. After six months, that's it, you know. Unless they took them out for another job, they could survive. So he did see that.

speaker: interviewee
text: He ran away from camp. When he ran away with another boy-- I have it in the book. I forgot his name. He ran away with another boy, and they were running and running. They didn't know where. They wanted to run to the forest to be in the Partisans, to meet the Partisans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the other boy, a German was after him. An SS recognized their clothes. They were yellow already, a little, at their faces. And he shot him.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he shot him, Shmulek ran in in the water, in the lake. And he swam through on the other side with his clothes. That was in the end of November, very cold, icy cold. And he was searching in Skarzysko to come to landlord, or people hanging clothes on the line, to sneak some clothes and to change, because he was wet. Anyway, he managed. He managed to survive going and hustling. And when we came back from work, he sneaked in. When he sneaked in to the line, you know, with the men, he came into the lager. Uh-huh, they did see a little boy, young boy, with a yellow skin. The Jewish police arrested him right away. His name was Tupperman. He arrested him. He was sitting in jail in camp. Now, they came in. They came to my bunk, and they told me all the news. Oh, God, I says. Now I'm losing my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the only solution-- what, what was his name, the fuhrer from lager? He was the command, the command, the commandant from lager, the Jewish commandant from the lager, was Mr.-- I forgot his name. It will come back to me later. You know, with names it's so hard to remember right away. I have it in the book, actually, and now I just remember. He knew us from Skarzysko. He was from-- It's not this name Tupperman you just said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tupperman was the policeman. The other one was Mr.--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll get to it later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And he saw my brother. He told him who he was. He came into my barrack and told me that he has little chances. And, you know, Tupperman, I mean, they [GERMAN] will come in, or not [GERMAN]. I mean the SS. What was his name? Bartenschlager will come in. And Kellerman was another one. And they will take him away. They will shoot him. Because it's not allowed to run away from one camp to the other. This is sabotage. It is forbidden. And he has to be-- he has to-- they have to get finished of him. So I says to him, look, you know my parents. Please. I had five brothers. He's the only brother. Please, try. He should survive. He says, well, I'm going to talk to him. The only thing I will tell him, he should lie with his age. He should say he's 16 instead of 14 or 13, you know, to make himself older. But he didn't look like 16. A 16-year-old boy, you could recognize. But he looked like a-- he look like a boychick.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he told him what to do. And because you have two sisters here, they will take care of you, to wash your shirt, or to be after you. And you want to work together with your sisters. And maybe you have a chance. It's the only thing what we can tell him. So I thanked him. We went back. He told my brother. And I-- they let me in to see him. He was tied in with a chain near his chair in the jail. My heart bleeded. And I told him, look, Shmulek, you have to say that. You have to remember your date that you are 16 and birthday. Otherwise they wouldn't believe you. And so they came in. And they looked at him. And they told him, [GERMAN]. It's a small boy. But by luck, by luck, they say, you want to work? Where do you want to work? I want to work with my sisters. And they, they gave him to work to my machine. So that was, that was the incident with my brother Shmulek. That, that's the way he survived. So that's how Shmulek, and you, and Tobcha stayed together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in camp. Until-- we stayed in the camp, all three of us together, until 1944. That was on November-- month of November. Didn't I give you the date, the date of what month? I think it was in June. I really don't remember. I know it was the end. The war was coming, you know-- it was-- rumors were going around. The Russians are, you know, fighting back. And soon the war will be over. But they still-- the fight was still going on. The fight of survival was still going on, how to survive, or how to sneak away, or to hide. But there was no opportunity for us. They took us to Leipzig. Over there in Leipzig, we had the same ammunition factory like we had in Skarzysko. We did the same work with the same routine, the barracks, and the appeals, and sending away people. And then everybody was afraid to mention that they feel bad over the little temperature. A new hassle again started. But the new hassle, of course, wasn't too long. It lasted only nine months. And the reading-- somehow we sneaked some old paper by the Germans. We had the German meisters. Instead of Polish meisters, we had German meisters working on the machines. Shmulek was sent to Buchenwald. And me and Tobcha slept together and marched with other three girls together and did the same work for nine months. Over there, over there we became-- on the bottom, we had also three stages. On the bottom, one, was one pregnant woman. And the other one was also pregnant. They came from Skarzysko. They had eventually-- Herlig-- Mrs.-- her name was Mrs. Herlig. She was a friend of ours. And the woman was from [NON-ENGLISH]. She was pregnant. The woman was pregnant. Of course she was from her husband. The other women, I really don't know. She just-- she was pregnant, but it wasn't-- she was from the beginning, her months, we didn't see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She was from Cracow, I think. But she came from our camp. Well, they were all withdrawn, quiet. Mrs. Herlig's lips were always white. I call her Herlig-- Herlig. In the book, I changed her name. Because her son lives in New York, and I didn't want to have nothing to do with her son. But I am the witness how his mother survived. And I will say that when I called him up and told him that I'm going to write the story about his mother, he didn't answer me. So I didn't want to mention the real name, you know. He's a lawyer now and in New York. He didn't want to do nothing, really. He was the second generation. I was very hurt that he gave me such a cold answer, you know. Instead to be-- to appreciate that I want to bring out the memory from his mother, he wasn't-- I didn't think that he was so happy. So I let it go. I changed her name to Herlig. Mrs. Herlig was a big woman, husky woman. She was tall. I knew her. I knew her husband. And-- but she probably-- she was in her-- when she came, she was in her fourth month. But what she did, she stole a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what means a [NON-ENGLISH]. We were wearing striped dresses. When we came to Leipzig, they gave us the wooden shoes, you know, the Holland shoes, they call it. They didn't shave our hair. They gave us the striped dresses, the prison dresses. Once in two weeks, they changed the dresses. We took showers, and they gave us other dresses from somebody else. Whatever they did, though, maybe they steamed them or whatever. The work was the same. Food was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this [NON-ENGLISH] that you mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stripes. There was gray.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're talking about just-- this was stripes on the dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, striped dresses, like in Buchenwald, the prisoners were wearing prison dresses. They were very rough material, you know, and like a heavy linen, very heavy. It was very heavy to carry them on your body. But we did. We got accustomed to it. That was our clothes, our dresses. We all looked the same. One looked, one looked on a short, a longer one, we all looked silly. But nobody paid attention about that. Mrs. Herlig, because I knew her, we paid more attention to her than the other people, the other inmates. She asked-- she stole a [NON-ENGLISH] dress, a prison dress. She stole it. And she gave Tobcha that perhaps she can help her to rip and make like stripes and wanted to bend around-- you know, to wrap around her stomach. She shouldn't show.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't know what was the reason. I says, Mrs. Herlig, why did you want to do it? She says, because I'm so fat, and I don't want my stomach to stick out. I says, but-- well, she did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: When she became-- like when she got to be in the sixth month and the seventh, she couldn't hide anymore, the other girl, too, the other woman, too. The other one was much younger. Mrs. Herlig must be-- she was a young woman, you know, about-- around 30 years old. And I-- Tobcha told me secretly that she is pregnant. I says, oh, God. They're going to send her away to Ravensbruck or to Auschwitz. She's not going to live. Tobcha says, no, she has no-- but you-- no, she has-- and the other one, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, appeal, everybody was on appeal. They examined us, two SS women. They touched our stomachs. They told Mrs. Herlig to the side, the other woman to the side, you whores, you bitches, you whores. You are pregnant.

speaker: interviewee
text: And their faces, they bent down their heads like you're taking somebody to be hanged, you know. And they stood straight, limping with their arms down. Their arms looked longer to me than ever. But their shoulders dropped down. I could not look at Herlig's face and the other girl's, too, the other woman's, too. She was young. She must have been in her early 20s, the other woman. And they waited in the barracks. They didn't let them work anymore near my machine. And a few days later, a truck came, and they took them. And more people from the ward they took it out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were not just Jewish girls over there. We were surrounded with Russians, you know, what prisoners, gypsies, you know, together. But of course, the Russians didn't do our work. They worked in the kitchen. They had better work.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the kapos. They did that. They gave them better work. Only we were the only one who worked on the machines, hustling from morning to night with a slice of bread, with a little soup, day in, day night. This is the routine from our work. As long as we didn't get hit, as long as we didn't get sick, that was a blessing, you know. If somebody got sick, there was very little opportunity, very sick I'm talking, little opportunity to survive. The sickness finished them up very fast.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the truck came, and by luck, because Mrs. Herlig is a big woman, a husky woman, they let her stand the last. All the skinny ones go in first to the truck. But meantime, the truck was loaded so full of people they couldn't close that door from the front. They told Mrs. Herlig, we're coming for you tomorrow. We're coming for more people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tomorrow never came. The Russians were beating very, very fast. Our camp, our ammunition factory, was bombed. The planes were going mad, back and forth lights, like you see July 4th. I did see-- July 4th reminds me so much of before the war ended. Because you see the same signs, where they're shooting-- they were shooting out from the airplanes, you know, signs-- red, blue, green. They gave them signals. One gave signals to the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we looked up. We said, my God, when will you come here? We didn't work. The SS-- we did see the SS talk to us different. It didn't take long. We thought that any day, they will come and we would be free. But it didn't come to the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meantime, Mrs. Herlig, the truck didn't come. And I says, Mrs. Herlig, every time when I went to work with Tobcha, I gave her a little cuddle on her shoulder, and I says, look, Helen-- her name was Helen-- Helen, look, have faith in God. That's what my father told me. Have faith in God. And I lived with that. I lived with that faith. And I says, you will see. You will survive. And she made it like this. She chuckled. She moved her head. And her white eyes, so stary, she says, yes, yes, I survive. They will come any minute for me. I says, no, they will never come. Now, next day, nobody worked. Mrs. Herlig is with us. The other woman went. That meant the other woman went. She was skinny. But Mrs. Herlig couldn't get in. And well, they took us out on the-- it was two weeks before liberation. But the last minute, they came in in a hurry. There were no time to talk. There were no time to discuss one inmate with the other. Hurry up. They gave us a half a bread, and line in five. The SS women with the rifles aroused from the barracks, and we marched.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can I tell you, marching? Marching in April? With the-- at night-- I will say hundreds and hundreds of women together in five. Well, for the first hour, you marched, for the second. But the second day, the third day-- at night we slept on the wet grass, one near the other. It was raining once, and the rain was falling on us. And we, and we turned with our back, and the rain fell on our back, not on the face, you know. Couldn't swallow the rain water.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we saw a river, we were running and picking up with our hands the water and drinking. When we saw grass high, we ripped out the grass and were eating. The half a loaf of bread was gone. We marched down for 10 days, from morning until evening. And then we slept on that grass, on that wet grass, every day in a different little town. For 10 days, this march was gone. Mrs. Herlig, while she was pregnant like that, her belly was noticeable. She was, she was in her ninth month already. She was kept on that ward with five very sickly people, which the truck could not arrive. They couldn't take them. Meantime, while we were going there, the Americans came into Leipzig, and we were marching. But every day, another city was liberated. They came into Leipzig, and they find Helen Herlig and these sick people. And she gave birth to her boy, to her son. In Leipzig he was born. And while we were marching, you know-- this-- the end I wouldn't tell you. She gave birth. But after I was liberated and coming to Feldafing and the DP camp, I was searching for Mrs. Herlig. I knew-- somebody told me that she survived and had a little boy. I came into her barrack, and then when I met Mrs. Herlig with her little son, it was a very touching, very touching coincidence meeting each other. But my 10 days, was-- mine and Tobcha-- Tobcha was-- because of Tobcha actually, I survived. She picked me under my arms, as she schlepped me. She says, you have to walk. I cannot leave you. I says, I can't walk anymore. I have blisters. The blood was running from my legs. No, you have to walk. I cannot leave you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked, and walked, and walked until we came to a station, to a little town. It was Schonfeld. The name, I mentioned, Schonfeld. And that little-- it was like a little town, like very little town to me. I didn't see no German people. Schonfeld, that was near Leipzig, not far away from Leipzig and, and, where else? And that-- the river-- it was-- I have written the paper there, the names.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, we were standing-- I did see that the Germans-- I didn't see no Germans. Every group went in a different direction. Tobcha says, well, let's say here. Meantime, it was Saturday. I did see the people standing with-- German people-- with their families. They want to go to the American side. And Elbe-- they wanted to pass-- they wanted to cross Elbe. The Elbe, the bridge from the Elbe, which the American people, they know, whoever was in the Army, the men, they know Elbe. They were fighting there in the Elbe. A lot of American men were fighting near the Elbe. And they bombed the bridge so the people couldn't cross, go over to the American side. They were afraid for the Russians, the Germans. They were picking up their families with their carriages, and with their horses, and with their wagons. And they tried to cross that bridge to go through the Elbe. But they couldn't make it. They got stuck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, staying and looking around, and I see-- I said-- I says to Tobcha, you know, Tobcha, I says, I don't see the Germans. I don't see our Germans. Where are they? She says, they left us, because they are afraid for their own life. You will see the Germans-- we will be liberated any minute. But where can we hide? The Germans shouldn't kill us. They were still wild, like, like animals. So we stood near a wall. Meantime, it was raining, so horrible, a whole night. We stood there, and then we find a wagon that was covered, you know, with a cover. It looked like linen.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got in after getting so-- being so cold and shivering from the rain. We got into that wagon. And we sat there for the few hours. But meantime, the, the, the, the shooting and the noise from the-- from the-- from the machine guns was so loud, like it's unbelievable. It felt like the wagon is turning over. The Earth is turning over. The lights were reflecting in different colors. The world is coming to an end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who cares? I'm shivering. I'm cold. I'm hungry. We're miserable. We were sitting like this, bent.

speaker: interviewee
text: Looking out from that cover, Tobcha saw a Russian soldier is digging a place near not far from the wagon. She says, he's not a German. He's a different uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tobcha was a very courageous woman. She had guts. She run down from that wagon and walked over to him. And she says, we are from concentration. What kind of soldier are you? Because you are not a German. He says, we are Russians. The city is going to be taken in the morning. It will be all Russians. Well, stay still. In the morning, you go down, and there will be a Red Cross waiting for you. It took us another couple of hours. We cheered, girls. We cried. We cheered. Some of them were depressed. Why? Why? The answer was why?

speaker: interviewee
text: We cried. There wasn't a liberation to be happy. You're going home. And you're going to tell your parents, I'm liberated. I'm going to see you. I'm going to be in the hands of your family, of your brothers and sisters. We know we are lost. We're lost shepherd.

speaker: interviewee
text: But yet, survival is very strong. How we had that courage and to be strong enough and wanted to live? Because I wasn't the only one. I was among thousands, you know, with people surrounded who were in my position. If I would be the only one, how many people in America ask me, how could-- how did you take it? How did you survive? How? Because I wasn't the only one. If I would be the only one, I-- believe me, it would be no way if I would be survive. I would take my own life, or I would die in a gutter, I would die in place. Who could survive with no money, no place, no future, nothing? I mean, it was strong life to survive, because one kept the other. I had my sister. I had friends, inmates, What we all kept together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what made us survive, our Jewish people. We're always together. That's our survival. One holds the other. One-- that's the life from our people. You know, from 2,000 years ago. We always marched. When Moses took out the Jews, we always marched together. We always kept, you know, that we should be together and live. Now, that-- this is-- when we survived, when I crossed, that was the most horrible thing, which could never fade away from my mind. I dreamed for years and years. I was holding my sister's hand, other girls together, holding their hands. This street in the morning-- it was about 6 o'clock in the morning. That street was covered with blood, horses, legs, heads from soldiers, Russians, Germans. It was the most horrible thing-- half faces, half bodies through and through. And I'm crossing, one leg here and crossing my leg there to cross over to come to the Red Cross, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop.
